basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Bayyina — “The Clear Proof” — comes from the verb bayyana (بَيَّنَ), to make manifest: that by which truth makes itself visible. But the sūra does not say that the Proof automatically lifts the veil; it says, more gravely, that it is after its coming that hearts divide. Light unites only those who do not resist it. At the centre of the sūra, two words from Tustarī illuminate everything else. On ikhlāṣ (إِخْلَاص) — pure worship, total sincerity: “All knowledge concerns acts, until the servant attains sincerity. When he reaches it, he attains profound peace (ṭumaʾnīna, طُمَأْنِينَة). From the one whose knowledge has become certainty and whose works have become sincere, God removes three things: anxiety, ignorance, and action — and gives him in exchange patience, knowledge, and the abandonment of choice.” And on the khashiya (خَشْية) of the final verse: “Fear is in the secret of the heart, while humble prostration (khushūʿ, خُشُوع) shows from without. The one whose limbs truly bow down — Satan does not draw near him.” The sūra, in its eight verses, thus leads from the Proof come from without to the sincerity within, and from sincerity to peace.

versets.ar: لَمْ يَكُنِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ مُنفَكِّينَ حَتَّىٰ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ
versets.tr: lam yakuni lladhīna kafarū min ahli l-kitābi wa-l-mushrikīna munfakkīna ḥattā taʾtiyahumu l-bayyina
versets.trad: Those who disbelieve among the People of the Book and the idolaters will not desist until the Clear Proof comes to them,
versets.commentaire: The verse, among the most delicate of the Quran, says that the deniers — whether among the ahl al-kitāb (أَهْل الْكِتَاب), “the People of the Book” (Jews and Christians), or among the mushrikūn (المُشْرِكُون), “the idolaters” (those who associate partners with God) — will not part from their state until the bayyina has come to them. The verb munfakkīn (مُنفَكِّين) means “those who detach themselves”, “who come loose”: their present condition will not leave them. The verse thus assumes a dignity in the denier: he will not be judged before the Proof has reached him. The Quran will repeat: “We do not punish before sending a messenger” (17:15).

mots.def: negative particle for the apocopated imperfect.
kāna: to be; here apocopated after lam.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: masc. plur. relative pronoun.
kafara: to cover, to deny; in the perfect plural: they disbelieved.
preposition: among, from.
ahl: family, people, those who belong to; construct genitive.
al-kitāb: the (revealed) Book; ahl al-kitāb: “the People of the Book”, Jews and Christians.
conjunction.
mushrik: one who associates partners with God, who practises shirk (associating).
munfakk: active participle of infakka, to detach, to separate; not to be munfakk: to remain in one's state.
particle: until, up to.
atā: to come; in the subjunctive imperfect feminine + plural suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: subjunctive imperfect + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

mots.def: bayyina: what makes clear, manifest proof; in the Quran, names the sign or the revelation that distinguishes truth.

versets.ar: رَسُولٌ مِّنَ اللَّهِ يَتْلُو صُحُفًا مُّطَهَّرَةً
versets.tr: rasūlun mina Llāhi yatlū ṣuḥufan muṭahhara
versets.trad: a messenger from God reciting purified scriptures
versets.commentaire: rasūl min Allāh (رَسُول مِنَ اللَّه): a messenger coming from God — most commentators recognise the Prophet Muḥammad; some also read here the archangel Gabriel, bearer of the Word. ṣuḥuf muṭahhara (صُحُف مُطَهَّرَة): purified pages. Ṣaḥīfa is the writing leaf; muṭahhar, what is rendered pure, washed of every stain. The image is concrete: pages are recited whose clarity nothing has altered.

mots.def: rasūl: a messenger, an envoy; the one who carries a mission from God.
preposition min: from.
Allāh: God, in the genitive.
talā: to read aloud, to recite; in the imperfect.
ṣaḥīfa: a writing leaf; plural ṣuḥuf, pages, leaves.
muṭahhar: intensive passive participle of ṭahhara (to purify); rendered pure, free of stain.

versets.commentaire: kutub qayyima (كُتُب قَيِّمَة): upright writings, enduring scriptures. Qayyim (قَيِّم), from the root q-w-m (to stand straight), names what stands upright, just, firm and without deviation. The purified pages contain “books that stand”: without errors, without distortions. The Word is upright there.

mots.def: preposition fī + feminine suffix pronoun: in them (refers to the pages).
kutub: plural of kitāb, book, writing; here, scriptures.
qayyim: upright, standing firm, without error; rendered here as “upright”.

versets.ar: وَمَا تَفَرَّقَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ
versets.tr: wa-mā tafarraqa lladhīna ūtū l-kitāba illā min baʿdi mā jāʾathumu l-bayyina
versets.trad: Those who were given the Book did not divide except after the Clear Proof had come to them.
versets.commentaire: The verse turns the movement: it is after the coming of the Proof, not before, that the People of the Book divided. The verb tafarraqa (تَفَرَّقَ) — to break into parts, to divide — names splintering into sects and schools. A famous ḥadīth: “The Jews divided into seventy-one sects, the Christians into seventy-two, and this community into seventy-three.” The irony is harsh: the light received did not unite, because it was received in a wrong way.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
negative particle: not.
tafarraqa: to divide, to break into parts; in the perfect.
plural relative pronoun.
ātā: to give; passive perfect plural: “it was given to them”.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: kitāb: the revealed Book.
exception particle.
preposition.
baʿd: after; in the construct genitive.
here subordinating: “the fact that”.
jāʾa: to come; feminine perfect + plural suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

mots.def: see v. 1.

versets.ar: وَمَا أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ حُنَفَاءَ وَيُقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَيُؤْتُوا الزَّكَاةَ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ دِينُ الْقَيِّمَةِ
versets.tr: wa-mā umirū illā li-yaʿbudū Llāha mukhliṣīna lahu d-dīna ḥunafāʾa wa-yuqīmū ṣ-ṣalāta wa-yuʾtū z-zakāta, wa-dhālika dīnu l-qayyima
versets.trad: Yet they were only commanded to worship God, devoting religion entirely to Him, as ḥanīfs, and to perform the prayer, and to give the alms. That is the upright religion.
versets.commentaire: The key verse. At the heart of the divine command, one word carries everything: mukhliṣīna (مُخْلِصِين), “rendering pure”, participle of ikhlāṣ (إِخْلَاص). Tustarī wrote about it sentences one never tires of rereading. Asked “what is ikhlāṣ?”, he answered: “It is responding (ijāba, إِجَابَة). Whoever does not respond has no sincerity.” Sincerity is not first an effort of purity; it is a movement of response to the Call. And it has three faces, Tustarī adds: sincerity of worship for God, sincerity of action for Him, sincerity of the heart for Him (ikhlāṣ al-ʿibāda, al-ʿamal, al-qalb li-Llāh). Three doors, the same threshold. ḥunafāʾ (حُنَفَاء), plural of ḥanīf, names the one who naturally inclines toward tawḥīd, away from every idolatry — so is Abraham called. Three practices gather it all: to worship God with a pure devotion, to perform the prayer, to give. dīn al-qayyima (دِين الْقَيِّمَة): the religion that stands upright — that does not bend, does not deviate.

mots.def: conjunction + negative particle: and not; with illā: “they were only commanded to”.
amara: to command; passive perfect plural: it was commanded to them.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: exception particle: but, except.
preposition li- (in order to) + subjunctive imperfect plural: that they may worship.
Allāh in the accusative.
mukhliṣ: who purifies, who renders sincerely; active participle of akhlaṣa. Root kh-l-ṣ: to be pure, free of admixture.
preposition + suffix pronoun.
dīn: religion, worship, way; in the accusative.
ḥanīf (sing.): one who naturally inclines toward the oneness of God, away from idolatry; plural ḥunafāʾ. A word often applied to Abraham.
aqāma: to establish, to perform (of the prayer); subjunctive plural.
mots.gram: subjunctive imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: ṣalāt: ritual prayer.
ātā: to give; subjunctive plural.
mots.gram: subjunctive imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: zakāt: the obligatory alms; the root z-k-w also means “to purify, to make grow” — giving purifies.
conjunction + demonstrative: and that, such is.
dīn: see above; in the construct nominative.
qayyima: that which stands upright, that endures; here, uprightness made religion.

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ فِي نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ أُولَٰئِكَ هُمْ شَرُّ الْبَرِيَّةِ
versets.tr: inna lladhīna kafarū min ahli l-kitābi wa-l-mushrikīna fī nāri jahannama khālidīna fīhā, ulāʾika hum sharru l-bariyya
versets.trad: Truly the disbelievers among the People of the Book and the idolaters shall dwell forever in the Fire of Hell. They are the worst of creatures.
versets.commentaire: sharr al-bariyya (شَرُّ الْبَرِيَّة): the worst of creatures. Bariyya comes from the verb baraʾa (بَرَأَ), “to create by shaping” — it is also the root of the Divine Name al-Bāriʾ, “the Maker who shapes”. The sūra does not say “the worst of men” but “the worst of what has been shaped” — all creation included. The next verse offers its exact opposite.

mots.def: emphasis particle.
plural relative pronoun.
kafara: to deny, to disbelieve.

mots.sens: among the People of the Book
mots.def: see v. 1.
see v. 1.
preposition.
nār: fire; in the construct genitive.
Jahannam: proper name of Hell, one of the Quranic names for the abode of punishment.
khālid: active participle of khalada (to last); who abides perpetually.
preposition + feminine suffix pronoun.
far plural demonstrative: those.
plural personal pronoun.
sharr: evil, worst; in the construct nominative.
bariyya: the created beings; what has been shaped, from the verb baraʾa (to create by shaping). Same root as al-Bāriʾ.

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ أُولَٰئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّةِ
versets.tr: inna lladhīna āmanū wa-ʿamilū ṣ-ṣāliḥāti ulāʾika hum khayru l-bariyya
versets.trad: Truly those who believe and perform righteous deeds, they are the best of creatures.
versets.commentaire: khayr al-bariyya (خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّة): the best of creatures. Qushayrī does not hesitate to draw from this a considerable saying: these men, he says, are better even than the angels — for the angels had no choice to make, no resistance to overcome, no falling and rising. The one who believes after he could have not believed, the one who acts uprightly after seeing within him the forces of the opposite — that one is honoured with an honour the pure spirits themselves do not share. The human condition is this possibility of the worst and the best; the two verses, set side by side, draw its portrait.

mots.def: emphasis particle.
plural relative pronoun.
āmana: to believe, to have faith.
ʿamila: to do, to perform.
ṣāliḥāt: good actions, righteous deeds.
far plural demonstrative.
plural personal pronoun.
khayr: the good, the best; in the construct nominative.
see v. 6.

versets.ar: جَزَاؤُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا أَبَدًا ۚ رَّضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِيَ رَبَّهُ
versets.tr: jazāʾuhum ʿinda rabbihim jannātu ʿadnin tajrī min taḥtihā l-anhāru khālidīna fīhā abadan, raḍiya Llāhu ʿanhum wa-raḍū ʿanhu, dhālika li-man khashiya rabbah
versets.trad: Their reward with their Lord shall be Gardens of Eden with rivers running below, abiding therein forever. God is content with them and they are content with Him. That is for whoever fears his Lord.
versets.commentaire: The summit of the Quran holds in seven words: raḍiya Llāhu ʿanhum wa-raḍū ʿanhu (رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ) — “God is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him.” Qushayrī comments: at this stage, “no claim remains that He has not granted them.” Every demand has fallen because every gift has been received, and every possible demand has been received too. On riḍā (رِضَا), contentment, he gives two definitions worth engraving: “It is the heart's joy under the bitterness of the decree.” And: “It is the heart's stillness under the flow of the decree.” The first says that no bitterness has prevented the joy; the second, that nothing fluctuating has moved the heart. The verse closes — dhālika li-man khashiya rabbah — with the condition: all this is for whoever has feared his Lord. Tustarī clarifies: khashiya (خَشْية) belongs to the inmost secret of the heart (sirr, سِرّ); the visible bowing-down (khushūʿ, خُشُوع) is manifest. “The one whose limbs truly bow down — Satan does not approach him.” And he adds: “The perfection of the fear of God is the abandonment of sins — those of the outside as those of the inside.”

mots.def: jazāʾ: retribution, reward; with plural suffix pronoun.
preposition: with, at, in the presence of.
rabb + plural suffix pronoun.
jannāt: plural of janna, garden; in the construct nominative.
ʿadn: abiding, lasting dwelling; a proper name, the Gardens of Eden.
jarā: to run, to flow; in the feminine imperfect.
min + taḥt + suffix pronoun: from beneath them, below which.
anhār: plural of nahr, river.
khālid: who abides perpetually.
preposition + suffix pronoun.
abad: eternity; in the adverbial accusative: forever, for all time.
raḍiya: to be content, to approve; in the perfect.
Allāh in the nominative.
preposition + suffix pronoun.
raḍiya: to be content; in the perfect plural.
preposition + suffix pronoun.
far masc. demonstrative.
preposition li- + relative pronoun man.
khashiya: to fear with reverence; not slavish fear, but respectful awe.
rabb + suffix pronoun.
